perturbation.original_text:
4. Term of Development Agreement.
4.1. This Agreement shall commence on the date specified in Exhibit "B". Unless terminated pursuant to Section 10 or 11 below, it shall expire upon the earlier of the date specified in Exhibit "B" or upon the opening of the last El Pollo Loco® Restaurant listed in the Development Schedule.
perturbation.changed_text:
4. Term of Development Agreement.
4.1. This Agreement shall commence on the date specified in Exhibit "B". Unless terminated pursuant to Section 10 or 11 below, it shall expire upon the date of the tenth anniversary of the commencement date, regardless of the Development Schedule or number of restaurants opened.
perturbation.explanation: This change introduces an in-text contradiction by altering the term of the Development Agreement. The original text states the agreement expires on the earlier of the date specified in Exhibit B or upon opening the last restaurant in the Development Schedule. The modified text establishes a fixed ten-year term from the commencement date, regardless of the Development Schedule. This contradicts Section 2.16, which discusses default due to failure to meet the opening date based on the Development Schedule, creating uncertainty about the actual term and obligations of the agreement.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.16. Developer also acknowledges that it is required pursuant to this Agreement to open El Pollo Loco® Restaurants in the future pursuant to dates set forth in the Development Schedule attached as Exhibit "B". If Developer fails to meet the opening date for any El Pollo Loco® Restaurant to be developed under this Agreement, Developer shall be in default and Franchisor shall be entitled to exercise all rights and remedies available to Franchisor set forth in Section 11. Developer acknowledges that if Developer fails to open El Pollo Loco® Restaurants in a timely manner pursuant to the Development Schedule, Franchisor will suffer lost revenues, including royalties and other fees which would be difficult to calculate and which Franchisor would have received had Developer met the agreed schedule or had Franchisor had the right to grant development rights to others in the Territory.
perturbation.justification: The modified text in Section 4.1 changes the expiration of the agreement to a fixed ten-year term, regardless of the development schedule. This directly contradicts Section 2.16, which states that the developer will be in default if they fail to meet the opening dates outlined in Exhibit B (Development Schedule). If the agreement expires after ten years regardless of the schedule, the developer may not be in default for failing to meet those dates, causing direct conflict and an in-text contradiction within the contract. The contradiction makes it unclear whether failure to meet opening dates will result in a default.

perturbation.original_text: 3.1. Developer shall pay to Franchisor upon execution of this Agreement a non-refundable Development Fee (the "Development Fee") equal to Twenty Thousand Dollars ($20,000) in immediately available funds, for each El Pollo Loco® Restaurant to be developed under this Agreement. The Development Fee is consideration for this Agreement. The Development Fee is not consideration for any Franchise Agreement and is non-refundable. The $20,000 Development Fee for each El Pollo Loco® Restaurant shall be applied against the initial franchise fee payable upon the execution of the Franchise Agreement applicable to such El Pollo Loco® Restaurant. As a benefit of signing the Development Agreement, the Initial Fee for the second and each subsequent restaurant developed under the same Development Agreement will be reduced by us to $30,000. As an example, the Initial Fee for the first restaurant developed under a Development Agreement would be $40,000 to which $20,000 (from the Development Fee will be credited. The Initial Fee for the second and remaining restaurants developed under the same Development Agreement would be $30,000, to which $20,000 from the Development Fee will be credited. If this Agreement is terminated pursuant to Sections 10 or 11 below, Developer will lose its right to develop and Development Fee.
perturbation.changed_text: 3.1. Developer shall pay to Franchisor upon execution of this Agreement a non-refundable Development Fee (the "Development Fee") equal to Twenty Thousand Dollars ($20,000) in immediately available funds, for each El Pollo Loco® Restaurant to be developed under this Agreement. The Development Fee is consideration for this Agreement. The Development Fee is not consideration for any Franchise Agreement and is non-refundable. The $20,000 Development Fee for each El Pollo Loco® Restaurant shall be applied against the initial franchise fee payable upon the execution of the Franchise Agreement applicable to such El Pollo Loco® Restaurant. As a benefit of signing the Development Agreement, the Initial Fee for the second and each subsequent restaurant developed under the same Development Agreement will be reduced by us to $30,000. As an example, the Initial Fee for the first restaurant developed under a Development Agreement would be $40,000 to which $20,000 (from the Development Fee will be credited. The Initial Fee for the second and remaining restaurants developed under the same Development Agreement would be $30,000, to which $20,000 from the Development Fee will be credited. If this Agreement is terminated pursuant to Sections 10 or 11 below, Developer will be entitled to a pro-rata refund of the unused portion of the Development Fee, based on the number of restaurants not yet developed.
perturbation.explanation: This perturbation creates an in-text contradiction regarding the refundability of the Development Fee upon termination. The original clause states that the Developer will 'lose' the Development Fee if the agreement is terminated. The changed text provides that the Developer will be entitled to a pro-rata refund, based on the number of restaurants not yet developed. This change directly contradicts Section 10.1, which states that the Development Fee is forfeited, creating ambiguity and uncertainty.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 10.1. This Agreement shall terminate immediately upon El Pollo Loco's receipt of Developer's notice to terminate. In such event, the Development Fee shall be forfeited to Franchisor in consideration of the rights granted in the Territory up to the time of termination. Notwithstanding any provision to the contrary contained herein, unless earlier terminated by either party, this Agreement shall expire on ______, 20___, and all rights of Developer herein shall cease and all unapplied or unused Development Fees paid pursuant to Section 3 hereof shall be forfeited to Franchisor.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 3.1 introduces a direct contradiction with Section 10.1 regarding the refundability of the Development Fee upon termination of the agreement. The original text and Section 10.1 state that the Development Fee is forfeited, while the changed text states that the Developer is entitled to a pro-rata refund. This creates a clear and irreconcilable conflict, making it unclear whether the fee is forfeited or refundable, thus meeting the definition of 'Inconsistencies - In Text Contradiction'.

perturbation.original_text: 2.14. RESAC site approval does not assure that a Franchise Agreement will be executed. Execution of the Franchise Agreement is contingent upon Developer completing the purchase or lease of the proposed site within sixty (60) days after approval of the site by the Franchisor or no later than the dates set forth in Exhibit "B" as Site Committment Dates.
perturbation.changed_text: 2.14. RESAC site approval guarantees that a Franchise Agreement will be executed. Execution of the Franchise Agreement is contingent upon Developer completing the purchase or lease of the proposed site within one hundred and eighty (180) days after approval of the site by the Franchisor, regardless of Site Commitment Dates set forth in Exhibit "B".
perturbation.explanation: This edit directly contradicts the existing language regarding site approval and execution of the Franchise Agreement. In the original text, site approval doesn't assure a Franchise Agreement, and it's contingent on completing the purchase or lease within 60 days or by the Site Commitment Date. The new text says that site approval *guarantees* a Franchise Agreement and extends the completion timeframe to 180 days, removing the relevance of Exhibit B's Site Commitment Dates. This directly contradicts Section 2.15 and Section 11.1.a where not meeting site committment date will lead to default. Thus, due to the modification made it creates uncertainty whether Developer is in default due to 2.15/11.1.a.
perturbation.contradicted_text: 2.15. Developer acknowledges that time is of the essence in this Agreement. If Developer has not obtained approval and entered into a binding lease or purchase agreement for each site for El Pollo Loco® Restaurant(s) to be developed under this Agreement by the applicable Site Commitment Date, Developer shall be in default of its obligations under the Development Schedule and Franchisor shall be entitled to exercise its rights and remedies under this Agreement, up to and including termination of this Agreement.
perturbation.justification: The changed text in Section 2.14 directly contradicts Section 2.15. Section 2.14 now states that RESAC site approval *guarantees* a Franchise Agreement and extends the timeframe for completing the purchase or lease, removing the relevance of Exhibit B's Site Commitment Dates. This conflicts with Section 2.15, which explicitly states that failure to meet the Site Commitment Date will result in a default under the agreement. This creates a direct contradiction regarding whether the Developer is in default for missing the original Site Commitment Date, as the changed Section 2.14 removes that commitment. This aligns with the 'Inconsistencies' definition: 'different sections of a contract provide conflicting deadlines, obligations, or penalties.'
